section.title: The Triumphal Entry
section.sectionRationale: Jesus enters Jerusalem as the promised King, humble on a donkey, to shouts of Hosanna. He acts with royal and prophetic authority in the temple, cleansing it and receiving the praise of children, and He curses a barren fig tree as an enacted parable of fruitless religion.
section.thesis: The gentle King foretold by the prophets comes to His city and His temple to claim them, purging the house of prayer, welcoming the praise of the least, and pronouncing judgment on fruitless, leafy religion that has no fruit for God.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the King's humble entry to Hosannas; the cleansing of the temple and the children's praise; and the withered fig tree with the lesson on believing prayer.

verses.text: As they approached Jerusalem and came to Bethphage on the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent out two disciples,
saying to them, “Go into the village ahead of you, and at once you will find a donkey tied there, with her colt beside her. Untie them and bring them to Me.
If anyone questions you, tell him that the Lord needs them, and he will send them right away.”
This took place to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet:
“Say to the Daughter of Zion, ‘See, your King comes to you, {{praus|gentle}} and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.’”
So the disciples went and did as Jesus had directed them.
They brought the donkey and the colt and laid their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on them.
A massive crowd spread their cloaks on the road, while others cut branches from the trees and spread them on the road.
The crowds that went ahead of Him and those that followed were shouting: “{{hosanna|Hosanna}} to the Son of David!” “Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!” “Hosanna in the highest!”
When Jesus had entered Jerusalem, the whole city was stirred and asked, “Who is this?”
The crowds replied, “This is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee.”

units.label: Jesus cleanses the temple
units.note: a house of prayer, not a den of robbers

verses.text: Then Jesus entered the temple courts and drove out all who were buying and selling there. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the seats of those selling doves.
And He declared to them, “It is written: ‘My house will be called a house of prayer.’ But you are making it ‘a den of robbers.’”
The blind and the lame came to Him at the temple, and He healed them.
But the chief priests and scribes were indignant when they saw the wonders He performed and the children shouting in the temple courts, “Hosanna to the Son of David!”
“Do You hear what these children are saying?” they asked. “Yes,” Jesus answered. “Have you never read: ‘From the mouths of children and infants You have ordained praise’?”
Then He left them and went out of the city to Bethany, where He spent the night.
In the morning, as Jesus was returning to the city, He was hungry.
Seeing a fig tree by the road, He went up to it but found nothing on it except leaves. “May you never bear fruit again!” He said. And immediately the tree withered.
When the disciples saw this, they marveled and asked, “How did the fig tree wither so quickly?”

“Truly I tell you,” Jesus replied, “if you have {{pistis|faith}} and do not doubt, not only will you do what was done to the fig tree, but even if you say to this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,’ it will happen.
If you believe, you will receive whatever you ask for in prayer.”

praus.gloss: Gentle, meek, humble. Matthew's word for the King of Zechariah's prophecy: this King comes not on a warhorse but gentle, on a donkey, strength under control, the same meekness Jesus blesses in the Beatitudes and claims of Himself, come to Me for I am gentle and lowly.

hosanna.gloss: A transliterated Hebrew cry from Psalm 118:25, meaning save now or save, we pray. Once a plea for deliverance, it had become a shout of praise; the crowds hail Jesus the Son of David with the very words Israel sang at the great feasts.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust in God. The withered fig tree becomes a lesson in believing prayer: faith that does not doubt lays hold of God's power, so that what seems immovable can be moved when His people ask according to His will.

basic.context: Jesus arranges to enter Jerusalem riding a donkey, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy of the gentle King. Crowds spread cloaks and branches and shout Hosanna to the Son of David. He enters the temple, drives out the merchants, and calls it a house of prayer turned into a den of robbers. He heals the blind and lame there and accepts the children's praise, defending it from Scripture. The next morning He curses a leafy but fruitless fig tree, which withers, and teaches His disciples about believing prayer.
basic.hermeneutics: The entry is a deliberate, prophetic act: Jesus stages a royal arrival that says who He is, yet as a King of peace, not war. The temple cleansing and the fig tree belong together; both are enacted judgments on a religion that has the leaves of activity but no fruit for God. Read the crowd's Hosanna and the leaders' hostility side by side.
basic.originalLanguages: The King comes praus, gentle, on a donkey. The crowds cry Hosanna, save now, the ancient plea become praise. And Jesus teaches that pistis, faith without doubt, lays hold of God in prayer.
basic.typology: Jesus enters as the Davidic King of Zechariah 9 and cleanses the temple as its Lord, fulfilling Malachi's word that the Lord will suddenly come to His temple. The fig tree stands for fruitless Israel, an image the prophets used.
basic.christ: Jesus is the gentle King and Son of David, the Lord who comes to His temple to purify it, the one worthy of the praise of children and the healer of the broken. He is the true temple and the fruit God seeks.
basic.god: God comes to claim His house and will not tolerate its corruption. He ordains praise from the smallest and lowliest, and He judges leafy religion that bears no fruit. He answers the believing prayers of His people.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 9 foretells the gentle King on a donkey. Psalm 118 gives the Hosanna and blessed is He who comes. Isaiah 56 and Jeremiah 7 lie behind house of prayer and den of robbers. Psalm 8 grounds the children's praise.
basic.oneStory: The King foretold for centuries comes at last to His city, gentle and lowly, to shouts of salvation. He enters His temple as its Lord to purge it and to receive the praise of the least, and He pronounces judgment on fruitless religion. The story now stands at the gates of the passion: the welcomed King has come to His own place, and the week that leads to the cross has begun.

context.literary: This begins the passion week, the final movement of Matthew. The staged entry, the temple cleansing, and the fig tree form a deliberate sequence of royal and prophetic action that provokes the leaders and sets up the temple controversies of chapters 21 to 23. The fig tree brackets the temple scene as a sign that fruitless Israel, like the tree, faces judgment.
context.historical: Pilgrims flooded Jerusalem at Passover, and messianic hopes ran high; a royal entry on a donkey evoked Zechariah and Solomon's peaceful accession. The temple's outer court had become a market for sacrificial animals and money changing, where Tyrian coin was required for the temple tax. Spreading cloaks and branches was how a king was welcomed. Fig trees in leaf normally carried early fruit, so leaves without fruit signaled barrenness.
context.canonical: The entry fulfills Zechariah 9:9. The crowd's cry is from Psalm 118:25-26, a psalm sung at the feasts. House of prayer quotes Isaiah 56:7 and den of robbers Jeremiah 7:11, both prophetic indictments of temple corruption. The children's praise is grounded in Psalm 8:2. The Lord coming suddenly to His temple echoes Malachi 3:1.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus as the prophesied gentle King entering His city and claiming His temple, to expose and judge a fruitless, corrupt religion, and to call His followers to fruitfulness and believing prayer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The entry and cleansing describe Jesus' unique messianic acts, not a template for our behavior. Yet they prescribe reverence for God's house and worship, warn against fruitless religion, and, in the fig tree lesson, call all disciples to faith-filled prayer.

debates.question: Why did Jesus curse the fig tree?

views.label: An enacted parable of fruitless Israel
views.case: The tree, full of leaves but without fruit, pictures the nation and its temple worship, all show and no fruit for God; framing the temple cleansing, the withering warns of coming judgment on barren religion.

views.label: A lesson in the power of believing prayer
views.case: Jesus Himself turns the miracle into teaching, if you have faith and do not doubt; the point He draws out is the mountain-moving power of prayer that trusts God, so the tree becomes an object lesson for the disciples.

debates.bearing: Both are intended: the sign judges fruitless religion, and Jesus uses it to summon His disciples to the very faith and fruitfulness the tree lacked, expressed in believing prayer.

study.originalLanguages: Matthew frames the entry with Zechariah's word: your King comes, praus, gentle, riding on a donkey, a King of peace whose strength is under control. The crowds greet Him with Hosanna, the old cry save now become a shout of praise, hailing the Son of David who comes in the name of the Lord. In the temple He acts as its Lord, and when the leaders bristle at the children's Hosannas, He answers from Psalm 8 that God ordains praise from the mouths of infants. Then the withered fig tree becomes a lesson in pistis, faith without doubt, that lays hold of God in prayer, the fruitful trust the leafy tree, and the fruitless temple, so plainly lacked.

typology.type: The King of Zechariah 9 on a donkey
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the humble Davidic King entering Zion to bring peace, fulfilled in the triumphal entry.

typology.type: The Lord coming to His temple
typology.fulfillment: Jesus cleansing and claiming the temple, fulfilling Malachi 3:1, and Himself the true temple, John 2:19-21.
Fruitless Israel and its worship under judgment, the image the prophets used, Jeremiah 8:13, Micah 7:1.

study.christ: Jesus is the long-promised King, but He comes gentle and lowly, on a donkey rather than a warhorse, revealing the character of His reign. He is the Son of David worthy of Hosanna, the Lord who enters His own temple to purge it and restore it as a house of prayer, the healer who welcomes the blind and lame, and the one who receives, and defends, the praise of children. In cursing the leafy but fruitless tree He pronounces judgment on a religion of appearance without reality, and He points beyond the temple to Himself, the true meeting place with God and the fruit that God has always sought.
study.god: God keeps His ancient promises, sending the King the prophets foretold, and He comes in person to His house, refusing to let it be made a den of robbers. He is a God who ordains praise from the smallest and lowliest, delighting in the worship of children while the powerful take offense. He will not accept leaves without fruit; fruitless religion falls under His judgment as surely as the withered tree. And He is a God who hears, answering the believing, undoubting prayers of His people according to His will.

crossReferences.note: Your King comes, righteous and gentle, riding on a donkey, fulfilled in the entry.
Hosanna and blessed is He who comes in the name of the LORD, the crowd's cry.
My house will be called a house of prayer, quoted in the temple cleansing.
The Lord you seek will suddenly come to His temple, fulfilled as Jesus enters and purifies it.

study.oneStory: The King promised across the centuries comes at last to Jerusalem, gentle on a donkey, to shouts of salvation. He enters His temple as its rightful Lord to cleanse it and to receive the praise of the least, and He curses fruitless religion in the withered fig tree. The welcomed King has come to His own place, and the passion week has begun. The story now bends steeply toward the cross, where the gentle King will reign from a tree and become Himself the true temple and the fruit God seeks.

quiz.q: What does the manner of Jesus' entry reveal about His kingship?

options.text: He comes as the gentle King of Zechariah's prophecy, humble on a donkey rather than a warhorse.
options.note: Verses 4-5 present Jesus fulfilling Zechariah 9:9 as the gentle King.

options.text: He comes as a royal figure welcomed by the crowds.
options.note: True, but it omits the deliberate humility, the donkey and Zechariah's gentle King, that defines His reign.

options.text: He comes as a military leader to overthrow Rome.
options.note: The donkey signals peace, not military conquest; He deliberately avoids that image.

options.text: He enters secretly to avoid drawing attention.
options.note: The entry is public and staged to fulfill prophecy, the opposite of secret.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus cleanse the temple?

options.text: Because His house of prayer had been turned into a den of robbers, and He came as its Lord to purify it.
options.note: Verses 12-13 show Jesus reclaiming the temple's true purpose from corruption.

options.text: Because He objected to any buying and selling near the temple.
options.note: The issue was corruption of the house of prayer, not commerce as such; this misses the deeper indictment.

options.text: Because the priests had asked Him to reform the temple.
options.note: The leaders were indignant, not requesting reform; Jesus acted on His own authority.

options.text: Because the temple was to be abandoned entirely.
options.note: He purifies it as a house of prayer rather than abandoning it here.

quiz.q: What did the cursing of the barren fig tree signify?

options.text: A judgment on fruitless religion that has leaves of show but no fruit for God.
options.note: The leafy but fruitless tree pictures barren religion, framing the temple scene.

options.text: A lesson about the power of believing prayer.
options.note: Jesus does draw that lesson, but the sign itself first pronounces judgment on fruitlessness.

options.text: Jesus' frustration that He was hungry.
options.note: His hunger is the occasion, but the act is a deliberate enacted parable, not mere frustration.

options.text: A promise that all trees would obey Him.
options.note: The point is fruitfulness and faith, not power over trees in general.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond to the children shouting Hosanna in the temple?

options.text: He accepted their praise and defended it from Psalm 8, that God ordains praise from children.
options.note: Verse 16 shows Jesus welcoming the children's worship as fitting Scripture.

options.text: He told the children to be quieter so as not to offend the leaders.
options.note: The opposite; He affirmed their praise rather than silencing it.

options.text: He ignored the children and focused on the adults.
options.note: He specifically drew attention to and defended the children's praise.

options.text: He agreed with the leaders that the praise was inappropriate.
options.note: He affirmed the children against the leaders' indignation.

questions: The King came gentle and lowly, not on a warhorse. How does the humility of Jesus' kingship challenge your ideas of power and greatness?
The crowds cried Hosanna, save now. What do you most need Jesus to save you from, and are you crying out to Him for it?
Jesus cleansed His house of what did not belong. What might He want to drive out of your own worship and heart to make room for prayer?
The fig tree had leaves but no fruit. Where might your life have the appearance of faith without the fruit God is looking for?
Jesus welcomed the praise of children. Where do you need to come to God with childlike, unguarded worship rather than adult self-consciousness?

translationNotes.note: The quotation is Zechariah 9:9; Matthew mentions both the donkey and the colt, and the crowd lays cloaks on them, highlighting the fulfillment rather than implying Jesus rode both at once.
Hosanna transliterates the Hebrew of Psalm 118:25, save now or save, we pray, which had become a shout of praise; the whole acclamation draws on Psalm 118:25-26.
Jesus joins two prophetic texts, my house will be called a house of prayer from Isaiah 56:7 and a den of robbers from Jeremiah 7:11, to indict the temple's corruption.
